title: Playing Dice with the Universe: Programming Quantum Computers to Play Traditional Games
abstract: The challenge of programming classical computers to play traditional, competitive games against human players has helped to advance classical hardware and software. Quantum computers have the potential to play games in a unique way: programmed only with the rules of a game, they should be able to implicitly represent all future paths of a game leading to wins, losses, or draws, and sample from this path set to identify moves that maximize the likelihood of a win. This permits skilled play without hard-coded or machine-learned strategy. As a proof of principle, we present early results obtained after programming the D-Wave quantum annealer with the rules of tic-tac-toe, enabling it to play against a human opponent. We anticipate that, as it has for classical computers, game-playing will serve as an important real-world benchmark for quantum computers.
summary: This paper presents a novel approach to quantum computing where game rules are encoded directly into a quantum annealer's Hamiltonian. By sampling the resulting state space, the system can evaluate the probability of future wins, losses, or draws without needing pre-learned strategies. Using Tic-Tac-Toe as a test case, the authors show that the D-Wave hardware can effectively play against humans. This work establishes game-playing as a potential real-world benchmark for evaluating quantum hardware performance.
main_problem: Exploring whether quantum computers can play traditional competitive games by using superposition to implicitly represent all future game paths and sampling them to maximize win probability without hard-coded strategies.
main_result: Demonstrated a proof-of-principle implementation on a D-Wave quantum annealer playing Tic-Tac-Toe, achieving a 100% win rate against a random opponent when starting first.
method: Encoding game rules and win conditions into a Hamiltonian using specialized logic gates (WNOT, WAND, PW) and using a classical outer loop to select moves based on the frequency of wins, losses, and draws in the quantum samples.
model_or_system: The D-Wave Advantage 2 quantum annealer, where game mechanics are mapped to a QUBO-style Hamiltonian using biases and couplings to represent move registers and win conditions.
key_observables: Counts of future game paths leading to wins, losses, and draws (n_win, n_loss, n_draw) and the estimated proxy probability of winning for each move.
important_parameters: Penalty values for logic gates (AND, OR, WNOT, etc.), number of samples per move (75,000 total), and the scaling of logical/physical qubits as the game progresses.
main_assumptions: The algorithm assumes a 'minimally strategic' opponent that always takes an immediate win if available, and it assumes the proxy probability from sampling is sufficient for decision-making.
figures_summary: Figure 1 compares quantum win probability estimates against classical exhaustive enumeration; Figure 2 illustrates specific game scenarios including exploiting mistakes, non-optimal paths, and losses due to lack of draw-prioritization.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the concept of quantum game-playing, details the Hamiltonian construction via logic gates, describes the sampling and decision-making algorithm, presents experimental results on D-Wave hardware, and discusses limitations and future scaling.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1: Probability of moves leading to a win (A) or loss (B), based on probability estimation by quantum annealing (cyan) or by exhaustive enumeration of possible games subject to the assumption of minimally strategic play (orange). Pale bars show individual corner or edge squares; solid bars represent the collective probablities for a corner, edge, or centre square. Note the quantum annealer yields P ′ (win|m), which is only expected to be proportional to the actual probability.

Fig. 2: Selected games played by the quantum annealer. Top of each panel: estimated P ′ (win|m) (cyan intensity) and P ′ (loss|m) (red intensity) for each candidate move based on annealing-based sampling. These are expected to correlate with win and loss probabilities by move, respectively. Darker squares have higher draw probability. White dots indicate move(s) with the highest win probability. (A) With a human player (orange) starting as X, the quantum player (cyan) playing as O appeared to play strategically, fully exploiting the human’s mistake in move 3 to force the human to choose between two ways of losing after move 6. (B) In a game against a randomly-playing computer opponent as O...
